data.title: PDO: Improved Earnings But Still Not A Buy
data.description:
Overview

As markets continue to trade near all-time highs, investors may be looking for a way to hedge against the uncertainty of the markets. The US government shutdown is still active, inflation is still trending higher, and markets are still trying to assess the impact of tariffs. An efficient way to diversify away from traditional equities is with a high quality income fund, such as PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (NYSE:PDO). When I previously covered PDO, I issued a hold rating due to the questionable dividend sustainability. Since my last coverage, the fund has released an updated annual report and macroeconomic conditions shifted a bit, so I wanted to reassess the fund's performance and outlook.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that PDO's share price has only increased by about 0.36%. What's notable here is that PDO's share price has remained stable in an environment of uncertainty around the debt markets. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 12.52% over the same period. PDO now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 11%. However, I remain a bit cautious about the overall sustainability of the distributions going forward.

Since interest rates remain elevated, I believe that PDO's growth may be limited due to the exposure to floating rate securities and the use of leverage. However, this is just the nature of these income-focused funds and I don't believe that is necessarily a reason for existing shareholders to sell. I think the fund would actually benefit from a small distribution cut because it would allow healthier NAV growth and higher total returns over time. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that PDO implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, PDO has total managed assets of $2.7B that is spread across a diverse range of income producing securities. The fund has a listed expense ratio of 5.22%, but this is misleading because it includes expenses related to interest and operating costs. Excluding these other costs, PDO's actual management fee sits at 1.15%. Although PDO's portfolio is primarily allocated to US securities, the fund also includes income investments within developed and emerging markets. The fund's breakdown by region is as follows:

  • United States: 71.41%
  • France: 5.43%
  • United Kingdom: 4.30%
  • Spain: 2.27%
  • Brazil: 1.81%

The fund's primary goal is income generation and it achieves this by utilizing a range of different assets. However, the top five asset types make up the bulk of the fund, with Non-Agency Mortgage securities making up most of the portfolio. These are private labeled mortgage-backed securities that are typically issued by investment banks. The fund's top asset types are as follows:

  • Non-Agency Mortgage: 22.52%
  • High Yield Credit: 21.54%
  • Non-USD Developed: 15.13%
  • Emerging Markets: 9.87%
  • CMBS: 9.32%

Expanding on this diversity, PDO allocates its assets across a range of different industries. For instance, PDO is most heavily allocated to Technology securities at 6.64% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Healthcare and Media Cable securities, which account for 4.93% and 3.91% of assets respectively. Despite the exposure to so many industries, investors should still limit their expectations around the fund's ability to provide a meaningful level of capital appreciation.

To demonstrate this weakness, I pulled in a performance chart of PDO since its inception. As we can see below, PDO's share price has declined by about 30.3% and failed to participate in the market rally over the last two years. When including all distributions paid out, the total return jumps up to 23.2% over the same period. Therefore, investors should expect that most of their returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions over time.

Updated Valuation

At the time of my last coverage, PDO traded at a very small premium to NAV of 0.57%. Following the recent market rally, PDO now trades at a higher premium to NAV of approximately 4.73%. For reference, PDO has traded at an average premium to NAV of 2.55% over the last three year period. As we can see from the red line on the graph below, PDO trades at the higher end of its historical range. Therefore, this may not be the best time to begin accumulating shares.

Since my last coverage, PDO released an updated annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $1.42 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.40 per share. Looking back on prior years, we can see that net investment income has remained within a consistent range, which indicates that PDO's strategy is efficient at generating a high level of income. What I like most about PDO is that net investment income is large enough to support the majority of the distributions being paid out. This reduces the fund's reliance on net realized gains to bridge the gap in earnings and can lead to greater price stability during declines.

However, the fund is still dependent on positive net realized gains to support NAV growth over time. The challenging part is that net realized gains can only be captured during periods of market growth, which allows PDO to sell its securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. This is why PDO experienced such a large net realized loss of $5.01 per share during 2022's market decline. This has stunted PDO's ability to recover from its initial NAV drop after inception. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $20.50 per share
  • 2022: $15.31 per share
  • 2023: $12.69 per share
  • 2024: $12.65 per share
  • 2025: $13.00 per share

Performance Risk

As part of the fund's operating strategy, PDO utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings when possible. The use of leverage currently amounts to 33.59% of assets, which is equivalent to approximately $900M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage during this period of elevated interest rates. A higher interest rate directly impacts the cost of borrowing for the fund and its own securities. A higher cost of debt means that PDO's operating spread between its costs and yield collected from securities are thinner, which makes it more difficult to grow earnings.

Additionally, investors need to anticipate the risk that leverage can amplify losses if allocated to securities that don't perform as anticipated. Since a large portion of the assets are allocated to real estate and securities that may operate on a floating rate basis, the fund has demonstrated a sensitivity to higher rates. As we can see below, PDO's share price rapidly declined as rates were increased throughout 2022 and 2023. Although the Fed initiated a 25 basis point cut, growth may be limited since the federal funds rate still sits near its ten-year high.

PDO may continue to underperform traditional market indexes over time, since its priority is on income generation. Therefore, I wanted to emphasize that a position in PDO only makes sense for the investors specifically seeking income generation within their portfolio. If you are looking to maximize your total return, you are likely to be disappointed in PDO's performance when compared against something more traditional like the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1279 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11%. The annual payout amount is equivalent to $1.5348 per share, assuming that payouts stay the same for the next twelve months. The updated annual report still doesn't increase my confidence that the fund will be able to sustain payouts over the short-term, as long as interest rates are elevated. The latest annual report indicates that earnings for 2025 landed at $1.83 per share, which is the first time since inception that the fund has generated a sufficient level of earnings.

While the payouts have remained consistent since inception, it has come at the cost of its NAV stability. Prior to 2025, PDO has been faced with the scenario of paying out more than it actually generates. While it's nice to see that the trend has shifted, I would like to see another year's worth of positive growth and dividend support. The good news is that net investment income has proven to be consistent, but I remain cautious about a scenario where interest rates do not trend downward over time.

Even in a scenario where interest rates do trend downward over the next twelve months, this doesn't automatically mean that growth will occur. A lower interest rate may translate to a lower level of interest income that PDO can collect from its securities. Therefore, management still needs to put an emphasis on new investment activity when conditions are ideal.

Since most of the distributions received are fueled by net investment income, PDO does not offer tax-efficiency for income investors. So based on your specific tax-bracket, the amount of capital invested, or desired income level, a position in PDO would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset the potential tax burden.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on PDO at this time. The fund trades at the higher end of its historical price to NAV range, so it may not be a good time to accumulate. Since the majority of PDO's portfolio is exposed to mortgage-related and other interest rate sensitivity securities, I have limited expectations around PDO's growth at this time. In more positive news, the earnings have improved for the fund and strengthened dividend coverage. However, I still remain cautious about the sustainability of payouts if interest rates do not decline over time.

data.title: SPHQ Does Not Beat The S&P 500, But It Beats It In Quality. The Paradox You Need To Know
data.description:
If you’ve read my latest update, you’ll know that for me the Invesco S&P 500 Quality ETF (NYSEARCA:SPHQ) deserves to be considered among the core solutions of a portfolio. The selection was naturally between SPY (the classic S&P 500 cap-weighted ETF), then SPMO (the S&P 500 momentum factor ETF), and some multifactor ETF solutions, either from Invesco or a few alternatives from iShares. From the analysis, it emerges that a multifactor portfolio fails to outperform a “handmade” multifactor combination between SPHQ (core component) and SPMO (satellite component), which is why my last rating was BUY.

However, SPHQ has clearly underperformed an ETF like SPY since my last coverage. So the question that naturally arises is: if SPHQ so precisely selects quality stocks, why hasn’t the market rewarded them? And above all, does it make sense to reconsider our ideas? Well, today I’ll explain my reasoning, and above all, why in my view, focusing on short-term performance can be a mistake.

A TL;DR worth knowing

For new readers, here’s a detailed yet structured TL;DR on the main features of SPHQ:

  • It tracks the S&P 500 Quality Index: an index that uses a selection ranking based on 3 key indicators, ROE, Accruals Ratio (which indicates the quality of earnings), and Leverage ratio.
  • The selection yields 100 stocks, included in the portfolio with a weight proportional to the quality score obtained, with a minimum weight of 0.05% and a sector cap of 40%, maintaining a semiannual rebalancing (typical of Invesco’s factor investing funds).

  • It has an AUM of $14 billion and an expense ratio of 0.14%, still interesting for a quality factor ETF.

Inside a portfolio

SPHQ has a beta higher than 0.8, thus getting closer to the performance of the S&P 500. The ETF therefore has a drawdown very similar to that of the S&P 500; in fact, in my opinion, an investor should not make the mistake of confusing “quality” with “defensive character.”

Taken alone, and extending the tf to 10Y, it has a competitive return compared to FQAL and QLC and the big advantage of having lower TERs and greater liquidity.

I confirm what I said in the last update: its strength, from a portfolio perspective, lies in its integration with SPMO (the momentum factor). In a 60/80–40 portfolio, it manages to combine quality, even when not yet recognized by the market, with momentum, that is, the market’s own selection. This also lowers the portfolio’s average P/E, considering that SPHQ has a forward P/E of 22.8x.

A combined solution that, in my opinion, remains more manageable than a classic multifactor ETF. As you well know, flexibility is an element that, in my view, should never be missing from a portfolio. Read here to learn more.

Today’s holding distribution

Starting from the sector distribution, SPHQ shows a marked underweight in technology and communication segments. In exchange, there’s a strong overweight in the industrial and consumer defensive segments.

And this is something we also notice in the top 10 holdings distribution. Within SPHQ, there are companies that you simply don’t find in SPY’s top 10 (and vice versa).

We've to recognize that this gives SPHQ less momentum.

Indeed SPHQ is not organized by market capitalization like a market-weighted ETF (such as SPY), and it follows a “quality weight” logic. This means that if the market rewards the capitalization of companies based on future expectations, SPHQ won’t capture them until they develop characteristics that allow them to be included in its top 100 (while the SPY does). Simply put, I think that’s the reason why it pairs so well with SPMO.

What slowed it down

In fact, the historical earnings growth of SPHQ’s holdings is above 10%, in line with that of the S&P 500, but with a ROE of just under double the S&P 500’s ROE, close to 60%.

So how is it possible that SPHQ’s stocks have been less in demand? If we follow the logic of GARP investing and compound earnings growth, SPHQ’s companies should be in demand, right?

The answer is “it depends on the market context.” SPHQ is less exposed to the tech sector and excludes the AI trade mega-caps from its top 10. But the market has heavily rewarded them lately, even valuing the info tech sector at P/E multiples of 30x; to be precise, that’s more than 92% higher than the average valuations of the last 10 years. Why doesn’t SPHQ capture them? Because SPHQ’s filters rely heavily on ROE, while the market in the AI segment has rewarded the operating margin of the AI mega caps. Essentially, they fell behind in SPHQ’s ranking. In my view, this also explains the lag during the rally.

My opinion

Careful, This is not a bad thing. Today, the sector that SPHQ is underweighting is also the most overvalued within the S&P 500 (info-tech). This simply means that SPHQ is not getting carried away, remaining anchored to fundamentals. And in my honest opinion, an ROE of 60% is a sign of structural profitability and can better sustain returns over the long term. At the same time, it still makes SPHQ a very interesting vehicle to combine with more aggressive satellite components, such as SPMO.

Risk

At the same time, underweighting segments such as communication and info tech would make it less attractive in the event of a continuation of the thematic rally. And the overweight in industrials and staples increases the risk of stagnation (in the short term, until rebalancing). Then, it should be remembered that the drawdown was not any better than SPY’s during contraction phases.

What I always try to remind myself, however, is that even if SPHQ does not protect against downside, the selection of quality companies helps mitigate the structural decay of the portfolio. In moments of high tension like this, there’s peace of mind in knowing you’re still invested in quality companies capable of recovery.

Conclusion

To conclude, I continue to believe that SPHQ remains an appealing solution, and personally one of the valid alternatives for a core portfolio component, to be integrated with solutions with alpha potential, more momentum oriented.

data.title: Heineken: Q3 Resilience, CMD Upside, And Attractive Valuation
data.description:
Today, we revisit Heineken (OTCQX:HEINY) in light of its Q3 results and Capital Markets Day. Spirits and beer have softened across the board, and most companies flag ongoing pressure among lower-middle-income consumers and certain groups — particularly the Hispanic community. Some headwinds are cyclical, but moderation is also emerging as a structural theme. Against these trends, our bias tilts to beer rather than spirits, and we like Heineken’s strategy to broaden reach among Gen Z and women while tapping emerging markets. Additionally, our buy rating was backed by its EverGreen strategic plan (updated today with new targets), 2) the premiumization trend in beer, and 3) a valuation gap compared to peers. Despite this, the stock has barely moved (Fig. 1).

Fig 1

Q3 Snap and our positive take on the Capital Market Day

Starting with the Q3 results, Heineken achieved sales of €8.7 billion, with net revenue down 0.3% on a quarterly basis (Fig. 2). This decline was primarily due to lower volume, which decreased by 4.3% and is down 2.3% year-to-date. As usual, Heineken Q3 provided limited financial details, but the company communicated its geographic operating sales trends, foreign exchange impact, and an updated full-year outlook.

Source: Heineken Q3 sales update - Fig 2

On a geographical sales review, we report the following key takeaways: 1) supportive performance in Vietnam with market share gain. Vietnam will see lower growth due to high comps, but will perform well in Q4, 2) higher competition in Cambodia, and 3) discipline in North America, with solid results in Mexico and Brazil. In Mexico, Heineken reported a consumer sentiment, with volume impact mainly driven down by the economy segment. In Brazil, there are destocking activities. As noted in our starting paragraph, the US has a tough market, especially among Hispanic clients. In Europe, the company is also losing volume. There are different trends across EU markets, but the company reported a hostile consumer environment.

The company is now guiding towards the low end of its Fiscal Year 2025 organic EBIT growth of 4-8% (Fig. 3). The outlook is underpinned by lower beer volumes, which are expected to be down modestly for the year. The rest of the outlook remains unchanged, with higher mid-single-digit growth in COGS costs and a €500 million gross savings target, which will be partially reinvested into marketing. In numbers, the US tariffs and FX will likely reach minus €290 million.

Overall, we view Heineken’s Q3 positively. Our last update had already reduced the 2025 EBIT forecast, even with lower guidance. Capital Markets Day also had several positive aspects.

Fig 3

This was an updated plan for the EverGreen strategy with a target of 2030. Heineken top-management targets mid-single-digit organic sales growth with EBIT growing faster (Fig. 4), underpinned by: 1) outperformance in global beer volumes, and 2) a step-up in gross savings initiatives to €400–500 million per year (Fig. 5). The message was a greater urgency on growth and a tighter focus on cash flow and ROIC. Management also acknowledged recent external shocks (such as Q3 LatAm softness) that could linger soon.

We are positive for the following: volume headwinds are cyclical, driven by weak confidence and affordability after pricing ahead of inflation, with the expectation to reverse this trend. The company will drive resource reallocation to 17 priority markets, which is expected to drive >90% of growth, aiming for sharper execution. Heineken also believes in a more moderate input-cost inflation, and with higher income, this will restore affordability. With the productivity savings, the company will reinvest in marketing to gain additional market share. Additionally, Heineken will likely concentrate 80% of its marketing budget on the 5 global and 25 local brands. We believe this will optimize growth opportunities.

The company is also progressing on working capital efficiency, pushing a higher cash conversion cycle. An incentive plan is now related to ROIC metrics (Fig. 4), which is supportive.

Although the beer volume recovery time remains unclear, we view Heineken’s CMD as a long-term positive, and today’s disclosures will be central to evaluating future performance.

Source: Heineken CMD plan - Fig 4

Fig 5

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Last time, we reported lower beer volumes, including stronger cost-saving measures. This is what has happened and what the company is forecasting. We do not expect a material gap between 2025 and 2026 sales, and visibility remains limited; accordingly, we continue to frame Heineken’s financials on 2025 estimates. Our call is on a recovery, paired with disciplined capital allocation that could unlock upside via shareholder returns. We see 2025 sales at €29.5 billion from a previous forecast of €30.75 billion and core EBIT at €4.5 billion. Compared to our prior analysis, we see better FX management, but it is immaterial; we previously reported a €310 million drag, while the company anticipated a minus €290 million result. We do not change our forecast for a delta of €20 million. The interest and corporate tax rates were reported in the CMD update at an effective 3.5% and 27-28%, respectively. So, our 2025 income reached €2.77 billion, and with the current shares outstanding, our EPS is set at €4.8.

Heineken is trading at 14.33x compared to a five-year historical average of 20.67x (Fig. 6). We used to value the company with an unchanged valuation methodology. This is due to consistency, so applying a 20x P/E, our price target reaches €96 ($55.5 in ADR).

Fig 6

Risks

We have long-standing coverage of Heineken, so the downside risks include a weak consumer environment and an unfavorable FX evolution (that said, the company anticipates lower headwinds). Higher COGS changes, such as packaging materials, malt, and sugar, impact Heineken. Additionally, any change in energy will negatively impact the company's P&L. Additional factors to monitor include higher corporate tax, advertising restrictions, lower cost savings, higher comps, and unfavorable weather conditions.

Conclusion

We are positive after the Q3 and CMD release. Heineken is well-addressing short-term challenges while aiming to grow in volume and market share over the next five years. This is also combined with a strict capital allocation that offers downside protection. At this valuation, Heineken is a clear buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TransUnion: A Quiet Compounder Poised For Long-Term Growth
data.description:
Introduction

TransUnion (NYSE:TRU) has an underrated business model of providing credit reporting to both businesses and consumers. By providing a scoring system useful to lenders, TransUnion has amassed a database on nearly one seventh of the global population and continues to grow in the double-digits each year.

Since I last covered the stock in December 2024, TransUnion has been a rather disappointing investment, having returned -7.5% over the period. That said, I think the valuation investors are paying today for the stock still makes sense and the latest earnings highlight that there is still growth to be had.

Top and bottom line beat

In its Q3’25 results, TransUnion delivered a strong performance amidst stable U.S. lending conditions with revenues coming in 8% higher and 7% higher on an organic and constant currency basis. At $1.17 billion, revenues were $36.5 million ahead of consensus estimates. On the bottom line, EPS of $1.10 came in above analyst projections of $1.04.

As for what led the growth this quarter, if we exclude last year’s significant breach remediation win, organic growth would have been up 11% which would have made it among the strongest top line performance exhibited since the pandemic era in 2021. As is often the case, the U.S. market (TransUnion’s most dominant market) fueled the sales engine with a 19% increase in Financial Services (12% excluding mortgage) and a 7.5% rise in Emerging Verticals. In the International business, revenues were up 6% organically with notable growth coming from developed markets like Canada and the U.K. but also emerging markets like Africa and India.

Moving down the income statement, adjusted EBITDA clocked in at $414 million, up 8% for a margin of 36.3% as revenue flow-through and higher volumes led to operating leverage. Interestingly, despite the international business being a smaller market with only one third the EBITDA contribution as the U.S. market, EBITDA margins were higher at 43.2% for the segment.

On the balance sheet front, TransUnion’s financial position remains strong with debt balance of $5.1 billion in debt against a cash position of $750 million in cash. On a net basis, that equates to a net leverage ratio of 2.7x which is down from prior quarters and is moving towards management’s long-term target of below 2.5x.

Given that the company’s credit ratings are solid at Ba2 from Moody's (with stable outlook) and BBB- from S&P, the company has access to a low cost of capital. Most of the debt is in the form of term loans with a weighted average term of 4.8 years with a current yield of just under 6% (source: Bloomberg).

Given the strong free cash flow conversion on net income (~70%), TransUnion has accelerated its capital return program, buying back $160 million in stock during Q3 and part October, bringing the year-to-date total to $200 million. The share repurchase authorization was also lifted to $1 billion, signaling confidence in the company’s value in public markets and potential undervaluation in the shares.

Key highlights from the quarter

In terms of some of the key highlights from the quarter, TransUnion management announced pricing for VantageScore 4.0. Starting in 2026, the company will price VantageScore 4.0 for mortgages at $4.00 per score. TransUnion will also provide VantageScore 4.0 for free to customers who purchase a FICO score through the end of 2026. Management also noted that the company will start providing additional alternative data like utility trade lines and rental payments, multi-year pricing for credit reports, along with a free VantageScore 4.0 credit score simulator for industry participants. All of this will be available through TRU's new TruIQ analytics platform.

While TransUnion did not mention pricing changes for the trended credit file report with a FICO score compared to a VantageScore. I’d expect similar pricing actions given Equifax (EFX)’s announcement on October 7th. I expect this will be justified based on TRU's willingness to include alternative data, as well as the VantageScore simulator, as these should all add additional value and justify a modest implied price increase to the core credit file.

Looking ahead

Going forward, TransUnion should be able to improve unit economics and avoid regulatory cross hairs. Lately, there’s been concerns from analysts on prior earnings calls about an expectation of the cost of the core credit file that might increase modestly in 2026, but I think that TransUnion providing multi-year pricing for credit reports will insulate it from FHFA criticism. Very likely, the incremental value of the services is probably more than the modest implied price increase and so I think additional options will be viewed favorably by regulators.

Taking this all together, management took the earnings call as an opportunity to raise guidance for 2025 which to me indicates that they’re confident in their ability to sustain high single-digit revenue growth and drive operational efficiencies. While India saw weaker growth at only 5% for the quarter, total revenue for the company is guided between $4.524 billion to $4.544 billion, reflecting 8% organic growth and EBITDA in the range of $1.622 billion to $1.637 billion, with margins at 35.9% to 36.0%.

Notice the third decimal point on TransUnion’s guide. Most companies wouldn’t be able to do this but TransUnion is and to me that speaks to the predictability of the business model and its ability to generate consistent, reoccurring revenue.

In the short term, the company is well-positioned to capitalize on stable U.S. lending trends and new business wins in its Financial Services and Emerging Verticals segments. However, India’s recovery remains a wildcard due to U.S. tariff impacts but I’m generally encouraged by management’s expectation of high single-digit growth for next quarter in Q4’25, as they see early festive season volume improvements and pro-growth policies from the Reserve Bank of India. Internationally, Canada, the U.K., and Africa’s double-digit growth trajectories should give investors confidence given the diversified geographies and mitigates the risks from softer markets like Latin America and Hong Kong which in their own right could surprise to the upside in FY’26.

Longer term, this is a great business that I think every investor should take a look at and potentially look to own. When looking out several years, EPS should compound at a 14% CAGR, doubling by the end of the decade. Driven by high-single digit top line growth, nominal margin expansion and share buybacks, the ingredients are there for investors to earn above-market returns on a name like TransUnion.

Some risks to consider

In my view, the primary risk my investment thesis on TransUnion lies in macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainties, particularly impacting its International segment. I’d never profess to be a macro expert, but it’s worth thinking through as an exercise to sport blindspots in a thesis. Right now, the 50% U.S. tariff on Indian imports has already dampened commercial lending to small- and medium-sized businesses in India (7% of total revenue), which led to the disappointing 5% growth this quarter. It’s very possible that ongoing trade negotiations or prolonged tariffs could further delay India’s recovery to its historical high-teens growth rate, but I think that long-term growth should accelerate. For the International segment overall, if we were to see weakness in multiple geographies (eg. Latin America with political uncertainties in Colombia), that would probably hinder the company’s ability to sustain growth and sales momentum.

Another potential risk is the competitive dynamics in the mortgage market where TransUnion’s push for VantageScore 4.0 adoption faces challenges from Fair Issac’s (FICO) entrenched position and its new $5 upfront plus $33 success-based pricing model. While TransUnion’s $4 VantageScore pricing is attractive, one could envision a scenario where lenders may prefer FICO’s lower upfront cost. If it ends up that consumers bear the backend fee, I think that would slow down VantageScore’s market share gains. There’s always black swan risk of a negative regulatory event given that there are only two other major players in the industry (FICO and Equifax) and any potential shifts consumer data privacy rules might disrupt overall industry growth.

TransUnion's growth justifies its valuation

Analysts are pretty bullish on TransUnion’s outlook and see value in shares today. Of the 25 analysts who cover the stock, 18 have ‘buy’ ratings with 5 ‘hold’ ratings and 2 ‘sell’ ratings. With an average price target of $105.64, analysts see 22.3% upside in addition to the 0.6% yield.

On valuation, I think the sellside is right to be bullish. When we look at TransUnion’s valuation numbers today, the company trades for just 18.2x earnings, well below peers at 27.1x earnings (source: Bloomberg). While one could argue that some of the names on the list have more defensible business models, I do think the margin of safety (in terms of risk to reward) is safer in a name like TransUnion rather than a name like FICO. Companies like TransUnion will probably continue to be around so long as credit scoring decisions are made and so even with competition, TransUnion (as one of the dominant market players) should hold some leverage in pricing and enjoys strong credibility in the industry.

Takeaway

TransUnion continues to deliver on its financial performance and so my thesis on the stock remains intact. Adid macro headwinds and some competitive pressures, I think the company's Q3'25 results showed that there's consistency and predictability in revenue and earnings growth going forward and so I feel confident in management's ability to deliver on guidance in Q4'25. With a growing international business with impressive margins and new product and platform offerings, I think the business should be able to deliver double-digit growth going forward. To me, that justifies the 18x earnings multiple on the stock today which I expect will compress as earnings picks up and buybacks continue. As such, I'm maintaining my buy rating.

data.title: Wells Fargo: Preferreds Remain Attractive After 10% Return In 4 Months
data.description:
Introduction

Wells Fargo (NYSE:WFC) posted a very robust set of results for the third quarter of this year, and the preferred dividends were once again very well covered. The guidance to increase the ROTCE to 17-18% from the current level of 15% is excellent, and that renewed the optimism. Meanwhile, the lower interest rates on the financial market have pushed the share prices of the preferred shares higher, which means the Series Z reported a total return of about 10% since my previous article, 4 months ago.

Lower Loan Loss Provisions Boost The Bottom Line Result

As I am mainly interested in Wells Fargo from the perspective of a preferred shareholder, I obviously need to keep an eye on the bank's ability to continue to pay the preferred dividends.

In the third quarter of the current financial year, Wells Fargo announced a net interest income of $11.95B, which is a 2% increase on a QoQ basis, and approximately 4% higher than the $11.5 billion net interest income generated in the first quarter of this year. The income statement below also shows the total amount of non-interest income continues to increase while the investment banking fees and card fees are on the rise. Meanwhile, the total amount of non interest expenses is increasing as well mainly due to higher personnel costs and higher advertising and promotion expenses.

This definitely was not an issue, as the banks pre-provision and pre-tax income increased to $7.6B.

As the image above shows, the total amount of loan loss provisions decreased to just $681M, which is the lowest amount in several quarters. This allowed the bank to report a net profit of $5.6 billion, of which about $20 million was attributable to non-controlling interests. As the image above also shows, the bank needed less than $250 million to cover the preferred dividends. This means the net profit attributable to the common shareholders of Wells Fargo was $5.34 billion, and this resulted in an EPS of $1.68.

It goes without saying that the quarterly dividends on the common shares of $0.45 are very well covered with a payout ratio of less than 30%. But from the perspective of a preferred shareholder, it is also very encouraging to see the bank needed less than 5% of its attributable net profit to cover all the preferred dividends.

The Preferred Stock Remains A Good Fixed Income Idea

Wells Fargo has quite a few different series of preferred shares outstanding, and as they all rank the same when it comes to seniority in the capital stack it doesn't really matter which series of preferred shares you buy. The only differing factor should be your preference to have exposure to securities that are more likely to be called versus owning a preferred stock with a low nominal preferred dividend, trading at a discount.

I tend to own a mix of both, as the share prices of preferred stocks with a low dividend coupon should go up as market interest rates go down. Back in June, I discussed the Series Z preferred shares, which are trading with (WFC.PR.Z) as their ticker symbol.

These securities offer a fixed preferred dividend of $1.1875 per year for a 4.75% yield on the principal amount of $25/unit. At the current share price of $20.11, the yield is approximately 5.90%, representing a mark-up of approximately 230 bps to the 5-year US Treasury yield.

One of the comments I usually see under my articles on preferred shares is that ‘5.9% isn’t enough’ and ‘there are plenty of other opportunities that offer a 7-8% yield.’ That’s fine, but there tends to be a correlation between risk and reward (an 8% yielder will likely have a different risk profile than a security with a 6% yield), and I am fine with a 6% yield considering the bank needed less than 5% of its net profit to cover these preferred dividends.

And secondly, I consider this type of security an excellent way to benefit from lower interest rates on the financial markets. In the past four months since the previous article was published, the share price increased from $18.54 to $20.11. That’s an 8.5% increase for a total return of 10.07% in four months and ten days. And that is one of the main reasons why I like this type of preferred securities.

Investment Thesis

I currently have no position anymore in the common stock of Wells Fargo, although the bank's financial performance remains very strong. I may write some other money put options in an attempt to get the stock at a slightly lower price. My main exposure to this financial institution comes from the preferred shares I own, which I still consider to offer an interesting risk reward profile thanks to the combination of a preferred dividend income as well as the potential to realize capital gains as interest rates on the financial markets move down.

I currently have a small long position in the Series Z preferred shares, and the large position in (WFC.PR.L), a ‘busted’ series of preferred shares with a slightly higher yield of almost 6.1% right now.

data.title: Blackstone Secured Lending: Disastrous Pullback On Dividend Cut Fears Swells Yield To 11.6%
data.description:
While Blackstone Secured Lending (NYSE:BXSL) now offers its highest dividend yield on record, coverage is extremely tight, and the specter of a dividend cut looms with the Fed set to reduce rates by 50 to 75 basis points through the next few months. BXSL is set to report its fiscal 2025 third quarter earnings on November 10, 2025, before the market opens. I expect both total investment income and net investment income ("NII") to grow sequentially from the second quarter, and coverage of the dividend to move above 100%. The BDC last declared a quarterly base cash dividend of $0.77 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $3.08 per share annualized for an 11.6% dividend yield. The yield had recently moved to its highest ever level since BXSL started trading on the NYSE in October 2021. Critically, the downward move of the common shares has been material, with these now trading below peak fear induced by the April "Liberation Day" tariffs.

The market expects BDCs to more broadly start cutting rates as their floating-rate loans, primarily backed by fixed-rate debt, look set to dampen investment income. BXSL's NII was $0.77 per share for its second quarter, providing an exact 100% coverage for the dividend. For the third quarter, I'm looking at origination momentum and comments made by BXSL's management to determine if the dividend will be covered. BXSL originated $631 million of new investment commitments during the second quarter, its lowest level of activity in over a year, with the trendline for this on a sustained back-to-back dip. Investment funding of $530 million versus investment sold and repaid of $185 million meant net funded investment activity was positive at $345 million, allowing BXSL to drive positive growth of its investment portfolio.

Originations, Yield, And Coverage

BXSL should be set to reverse this trend, with management stating during their second quarter earnings call that they expect deal activity to pick up meaningfully in the second half of 2025. Further, the 25 basis points rate cut did not come until September 17, around 2 weeks before the end of the third quarter. This will mean a more muted impact on the BDC's weighted average yield on performing debt and income-producing investments at fair value. This was flat sequentially at 10.2% during the second quarter, albeit down by 140 basis points on a year-over-year basis.

BXSL's second quarter investments at fair value of $13.25 billion grew by $419 million versus the first quarter. I expect another sequential growth in the third quarter, with the weighted average yield on performing debt and income-producing investments coming in broadly flat to drive incremental NII per share growth. BXSL's NII has declined for three consecutive quarters and is down from a peak of $1.06 per share in the second quarter of fiscal 2023.

There has been a clear split in strategy between BDCs that are pushing record origination volume to counter falling prime rates, with Hercules Capital (HTGC) notching record gross originations and fundings during its second quarter. U.S. GDP is forecast to grow by 3% in the third quarter, with pending back-to-back Fed rate cuts set to boost aggregate demand in the economy. Hence, I don't think the U.S. economy will experience the type of macro headwinds implied by BXSL's more prudent gross originations cadence over the last year. While a cut might seem inevitable for a range of BDCs at this point, constraining its broad scope and scale is important, and a larger credit portfolio represents the primary way a BDC can achieve this without moving towards riskier second-lien credit.

Conclusion

It's important to note that BXSL had undistributed spillover income of $1.86 per share as of the end of its second quarter, which could provide a bridge for the BDC to maintain its dividend through next year. I highlighted some uncertainty around whether BXSL would tap this to plug any shortfalls when I last covered the ticker. BXSL stated during its most recent earnings call that covering any shortfalls will happen in the short-term, but not in the long-term. The BDC also held some floating-rate debt anchored to SOFR that should also benefit from rate cuts. I think the market has overreacted here, with the ongoing selloff in part due to credit concerns around the failure of First Brands Group and Tricolor. BXSL has zero exposure to these two junk-rated companies, as do the majority of BDCs. BXSL's loans on non-accrual status were just 0.3% of its investments at cost. What should have been clear examples of bad underwriting and idiosyncratic risk have been extrapolated across the broader private credit space as a tradable event. This is the current opportunity with BXSL's common shares. We likely won't get a dividend cut in the fourth quarter, and the commons could still recover as this tradeable event unwinds even as the Fed cuts rates.

data.title: Microsoft, Meta Set To Report Earnings As Investors Watch Out For Trump-Xi, Fed Meeting
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street opened on a positive note Friday, with the S&P 500 hitting a record intraday high after September's consumer price index came in slightly cooler than expected. The long-delayed report, postponed by the ongoing U.S. government shutdown, showed CPI rising 0.3% month-over-month-below both the 0.4% consensus and August's 0.4% increase. Investor sentiment was further lifted by strong results from Intel, whose shares jumped 5.7% in early trading after the chipmaker posted third-quarter earnings and guidance that beat expectations. The upbeat open followed Thursday's gains, when markets rallied as investors digested Tesla's results and news that President Donald Trump will meet with President Xi next Thursday.

Amid a U.S. government shutdown, investors will keep a close eye on economic reports that will be released next week; however, government numbers are in doubt as the shutdown continues. The economic reports that are expected to be announced next week include reports on Core Durable Goods Orders, Dallas Fed Mfg Business Index, and Atlanta Fed GDP, with reports on Redbook and Richmond Manufacturing Index releasing on Tuesday. Wednesday will see the release of MBA Mortgage Applications, Pending Home Sales, and the FOMC Interest Rate Statement, with Core PCE Prices releasing on Thursday. Employment Benefits and Personal Spending are the main reports scheduled to be announced on Friday.

Apple (AAPL), Amazon.com (AMZN), Eli Lilly (LLY), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG), Meta Platforms (META), Caterpillar (CAT), ServiceNow (NOW), Boeing (BA), Exxon Mobil (XOM), AbbVie (ABBV), Chevron (CVX), Charter Communications (CHTR), Visa (V), and UnitedHealth (UNH) will report earnings next week.

_____________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, Oct 27: Waste Management (WM), Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP), Brown & Brown (BRO), FTAI Aviation (FTAI), F5 Networks (FFIV). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, Oct 28: Visa, UnitedHealth, NextEra Energy (NEE), Booking (BKNG), United Parcel Service (UPS), PayPal (PYPL), MSCI (MSCI). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, Oct 29: Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta Platforms, Caterpillar, ServiceNow, Boeing. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, Oct 30: Apple, Amazon.com, Eli Lilly, Mastercard (MA), Reddit (RDDT). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday, Oct 31: Exxon Mobil, AbbVie, Chevron, Charter Communications. See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Inhibrx Biosciences (INBX) and Deckers Outdoor (DECK) are set up for a volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Investview (OTCQB:INVU), Armata Pharmaceuticals (ARMP), and Tourmaline Bio (TRML). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Integer (ITGR), Ready Capital (RC), and United Homes Group (UHG). Beyond Meat (BYND) and Kohl's (KSS) head into the week with a high level of short interest outstanding and social media buzz active.

IPO watch: Companies expected to price their IPOs and begin trading next week include clinical-stage biopharmaceutical firm MapLight Therapeutics (MPLT), HW Electro KK (HWEP), and travel tech company Navan (NAVN).

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Realty Income (O), Morgan Stanley (MS), Cal-Maine (CALM), and Citizens Financial (CFG).

Microsoft Earnings Preview: Microsoft is set to announce its first-quarter earnings next week, with analysts expecting the company to report earnings per share of $3.66 on revenue of $75.37B. Analysts expect another clean beat and little room for disappointment. All eyes are on Azure's reacceleration as AI workloads turn into real demand despite ongoing capacity limits. Analysts expect CapEx to remain elevated but strategically deployed, keeping the long-term AI narrative credible rather than inflated.

Meta Earnings Preview: Analysts expect Meta to report earnings per share of $6.64 on revenue of $49.36B, with a primary focus on the AI market and the company's AI monetization. Meta's broader trend remains underpinned by investor confidence in its AI expansion and data center build-out. Meanwhile, the partnership with Arm comes at a crucial time for Meta, which has faced a volatile trading month amid market-wide profit-taking in large-cap tech. This pivot is increasingly significant as the competition in generative and recommendation-based AI intensifies, with peers like Google and Amazon also ramping up infrastructure spending.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Retire On Income: Revealing 2 Of My Holdings Up To 9% Cash Per Year
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

"What is Jerusalem worth?" - Balian of Ibelin

"Nothing." - Saladin

Takes a few steps, raises his arms, smiles, and says

"Everything."

That famous scene from Kingdom of Heaven captures a fundamental element in human psychology: Value is never fixed; it is defined by circumstance.

Can something really be worth both nothing and everything at the same time? It is always circumstantial. Food at the airport costs triple what it would anywhere else. Similarly, popcorn at a theater or theme park sells for 10-20 times its actual value. You would spend $2.99/Gal to fill your tank at your neighborhood gas station, but if you are stranded with an empty tank in the middle of nowhere (and you didn't invest in a good roadside assistance plan), prepare to pay whatever they ask. It would only cost a few bucks to make a spare key at Home Depot. However, losing your only key at 11 p.m. will require you to pay a locksmith a small fortune.

Value is never absolute. It's relative to timing, need, and circumstance.

Retirement is no different.

When you retire, you stop receiving regular paychecks from your employer, but you still need income to tackle bills, maintain your lifestyle, and handle surprise expenses. If your retirement plan depends on you manually selling shares at regular intervals, then market volatility becomes a source of anxiety.

That's why at High Dividend Opportunities, we focus on steady, reliable income from essential market sectors. Prices go up and down, but I can count on my portfolio to produce the cash flow I need all the time, not just when the market sentiment is bullish. With that in mind, let's look at our top picks.

Pick #1: CIM Baby Bonds - Up To 9% Yields

Chimera Investment Corp. (CIM) is an mREIT focused primarily on a diversified portfolio of mortgage assets, particularly residential mortgage loans, agency and non-agency MBS (Mortgage Backed Securities), and MSR (Mortgage Servicing Rights). As of June 30, 2025, CIM's assets were allocated 74% to residential loans (including 66% in reperforming loans, which are loans that were previously delinquent but brought back to current standing), 19% to Agency MBS, and 7% to Non-Agency RMBS. Source

The mREIT places significant emphasis on re-performing loans. As such, the average FICO score of its residential loan portfolio stood at 669 at the end of Q2. However, CIM maintains exposure to over 99,600 loans, with a weighted-average maturity of 20.5 years, and average loan-to-value at origination of 77.6% (and 63% amortized). About 83% of CIM's loans are issued against properties that are owner-occupied.

The mREIT ended the second quarter with $250 million in cash and cash equivalents, and the company announced the acquisition of a leading non-qualified mortgage lender, HomeXpress, to be in a position to create and originate their own mortgages, while controlling the quality and loan terms. This bolt-on acquisition, which was completed on October 1st, will also allow the mREIT to generate more securitizations and invest in them, instead of only buying them.

In recent years, CIM has been deleveraging well, bringing its total leverage down to 4.5x at the end of the second quarter. During the Q2 conference call, management noted their intent to diversify their income streams, and grow assets, to support and grow their dividend over time.

"We'll continue diversifying our portfolio and income streams, growing recurring fee income, adding liquidity and looking for opportunities to add accretive platforms and invest in accretive assets, all with the focus of growing our assets and dividend or total economic return over the long term." - Phillip John Kardis, President & CEO

We are interested in CIM's three public baby bonds, which offer high yields while presenting excellent safety for investors.

  • 9.000% Senior Notes due 5/15/2029 (CIMN) - Yield 8.9%

  • 9.250% Senior Notes due 8/15/2029 (CIMO) - Yield 9%

  • 8.875% Senior Notes due 8/15/2030 (CIMP) - Yield 8.8%

CIM's baby bonds carry BBB ratings from Egan-Jones, with maturities in 2029 and 2030. CIMN and CIMO become callable next year, in May and August, respectively; while CIMP isn't callable until August 2027, providing a runway for steady income. CIMP presents the best opportunity for investors at this time.

CIM spent $42.7 million on preferred dividends during 1H 2024, covered by $202 million in net income (which is calculated after factoring in interest expenses). The mREIT's liquidity position is strong enough to support business growth and shareholder/debtholder commitments.

Pick #2: SCHW Preferreds - Up To 6% Yields

Charles Schwab (SCHW) is the largest U.S. retail broker, with 45 million client accounts and $11.6 trillion in client assets as of October 2025. The company offers its individual and institutional clients a wide range of financial services, like banking, brokerage, wealth management, and advisory services.

SCHW recently reported a solid third quarter, with a 14% YoY increase in total AUM (Assets Under Management), a whopping 39% YoY increase ($80.3 billion) in core net new assets. SCHW company benefits from increased market volatility through higher trading revenues. During Q3, the company facilitated 7.4 million daily average trades and reported a 25% YoY increase in trading revenues. On the other side of this, SCHW enjoys steady fee-based revenues in its wealth and asset management segments, with 13% YoY growth in administration and management fees to $1.7 billion.

During the third quarter, the company spent $2.7 billion to repurchase 28.9 million shares, and this brings the YTD total to $8.5 billion towards share repurchases. With this, the company still maintains adequate capacity from its authorized $20 billion share repurchase plan.

SCHW has been a terrific dividend steward since its inception, with periodic payout increases. However, its current annual yield stands at a paltry 1.1%, making it less desirable for our passive income needs.

We will explore higher in the company's financial structure, for greater yields at discounted price points. SCHW has two public perpetual preferreds, rated "BBB" by S&P, and offering yields of up to 5.9%.

  • 5.95% Non-Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock Series D (SCHW.PR.D) - Yield 5.9%

  • 4.45% Non-Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock Series J (SCHW.PR.J) - Yield 5.6%

About $343 million was spent on preferred stock dividends during the first nine months of 2025, while generating $6 billion in net income during this period (SCHW reported this net income after factoring in preferred dividend expenses). At this time, SCHW-J offers a CD-beating 5.6% QDI yield, and ~25% upside to par.

SCHW prefers to enjoy excellent coverage from the company's predictably growing operations, and is well-positioned for upside with interest-rate cuts.

Conclusion

Valuation is an art, not a science. And value is always determined by circumstance. Whether it is a snack, a tank of gas, or a spare key, none of these command premium pricing until desperation or lack of options strikes. Your retirement income works the same way.

During good times, companies provide healthy forecasts, and the markets are jumping in excitement. Investors consider themselves geniuses and convince themselves that selling shares will always be a reasonable, available, and sustainable option. However, it is important to remember that most investors today haven't seen a serious recession for decades. It is easy to say things have evolved and that the government will come to the rescue of the markets, or that this time is different. You say whatever you want to convince yourself that stocks only go up. When volatility strikes, or when life throws an unexpected bill your way, your ability to rely on your portfolio for reliable cash flow becomes the difference between confidence and panic.

At High Dividend Opportunities, our focus isn't on predicting the next rally or timing exits. It's on securing income from sectors that provide essential functions and have proven their resilience across economic cycles. Reliable income turns uncertainty into choice, and it is what every retiree truly values.

data.title: Intesa: Not Much Value Beyond Its 6.2% Dividend Yield
data.description:
As I’ve covered in the past, Intesa Sanpaolo (OTCPK:ISNPY)(OTCPK:IITSF) is a quality bank in the European banking sector, plus it historically offered a high-dividend yield that was quite attractive to income-oriented investors. Since my last article on the bank, some couple of years ago, its share price performance has been quite impressive, being up by more than 190% including dividends, beating the overall stock market during the same period by a wide margin.

As I’ve not covered Intesa for some time and the bank is scheduled to report its quarterly earnings related to Q3 2025 in the coming days, I think it’s now a good time to revisit its investment case and update its financial performance, to see if it still offers value for investors following a strong share price rally in recent years.

Earnings Analysis

Intesa has reported a positive operating performance over the past few quarters, despite headwinds from lower market rates due to the European Central Bank (ECB) declining rate path from the middle of 2024 to last June. As shown in the next graph, the ECB cut several times its key rate from a peak level of 4.5% in 2024, to its current level of 2.15% that has been unchanged over the past few months.

While Intesa is not among the European banks more exposed to rates, as the bank’s diversification strategy has resulted in a diversified revenue mix compared to some other banks, it’s not immune to lower market rates. Moreover, contrary to other European countries where loans are largely set at fixed-rates, variable-rate loans dominate in Italy. This makes banks in the country more exposed to changes in interest rates than banks operating in Central European countries or Scandinavia, for instance.

This explains why Intesa’s net interest income (NII) declined by 6.8% YoY during the first half of 2025 (H1 2025) to €7.4 billion, showing that rates are now a headwind for the bank’s top-line performance. However, fees, commissions, and insurance income performed well, delivering higher revenues in H1 2025 compared to the same period last year. Its overall revenues amounted to €13.8 billion in the first semester (+1.1% YoY), showing that strength in other revenues was enough to offset NII weakness. NII’s weight on total revenues was about 54% in the period, showing that Intesa is not very reliant on NII compared to other retail-oriented banks (which have weights above 75%), but its variable-rate loan book makes it exposed to declining market rates.

During Q3 2025, the Euribor rate (which usually is used as the benchmark for variable-rate loans) traded sideways between 1.95%-2.05%, which means the bank’s NII is likely to be more stable on a quarterly basis than in the previous quarters. Indeed, according to analysts’ estimates, its NII should be around €3.7 billion in Q3 2025, down by just 2% quarter-on-quarter, while on an annual basis this represents a decline of 3.8%. For the following quarters, the street expects Intesa’s NII to be between €3.7-3.8 billion in Q4 and through 2026, as the market is assuming a stable ECB rate until the end of next year.

Regarding other revenue lines, the street is forecasting some growth on fees and commissions over the next few quarters, but lower income from insurance operations, showing that Intesa’s revenue growth prospects are somewhat muted. Its quarterly revenues are expected to be around €7 billion over the next four quarters, not being very different from what the bank has reported in Q2 2025.

Regarding costs, the bank has reported good cost control during a period of inflationary pressures, which has pushed staff costs higher in recent quarters. Despite that, Intesa’s operating expenses declined by 0.2% YoY to €5.24 billion in H1 2025, leading to positive operating jaws (revenue growth minus cost growth) in the period. This was positive for a slight improvement in the bank’s operating efficiency, measured by the cost-to-income (C/I) ratio that declined by 50 basis points (bps) to 38% in Q2. This is a very good level of efficiency compared to other European banks, being justified by Intesa’s investments in digitalization and rationalization of its branch network in recent years, but also by seasonal factors (high-interest rates in recent years).

Due to this combination of slightly higher revenues and good cost control, its pre-provision profit increased by 1.9% YoY to more than €8.5 billion in the first half of 2025, which can be considered a good result considering that rates are now a headwind for earnings growth. However, over the next few quarters, its pre-provision profit is expected to be relatively flat compared to its recent history, reflecting the bank’s muted growth prospects and top-line headwind from lower rates.

Regarding its credit quality, it has remained quite good as the Italian economy has been resilient, being positive for the credit performance of consumers and SMEs. Reflecting good credit quality despite some macroeconomic uncertainties, including, for instance, the impact of the U.S. trade war on Italian exports, Intesa’s provisions for loan losses declined by 8.8% YoY, to €505 million in H1 2025.

Given this backdrop of slightly higher operating income and lower provisions for loan losses, plus lower extraordinary costs (lower by €236 million in the first semester compared to H1 2024) related to integration costs and exit incentives for staff, Intesa’s net income increased by 9% YoY to more than €5.2 billion in H1 2025. This is a new record for the first half of the year, showing that Intesa delivered a good performance during a tough period, with some positive effects offsetting the negative impact of lower rates in the bank’s NII.

Given this resilient performance, Intesa raised its guidance for the full year, expecting now to deliver a net income well above €9 billion. This represents annual growth of at least 4% YoY, while the street is expecting a net income of about €9.3 billion in 2025 (+7% YoY). This means its net income is expected to decline in the second half of the year compared to H1 2025, which is explained by the street estimating higher provisions for loan losses in Q3 and Q4. Indeed, for the second half of the year, provisions are expected to be around €850 million, up by 68% from the previous semester, justified by weaker economic conditions expected ahead and some impact of U.S. tariffs on Italian corporations. Additionally, the bank usually books in Q4 the impact of restructuring measures, namely friendly staff exits, that are expected to result in a lower net income in the second half of the year. Despite that, Intesa’s profitability is expected to remain at very high levels given that its return on equity (ROE) ratio was 20% in H1 2025 and is expected to be around 15% for the full year (vs. 17% in 2024), being a very good profitability level within the European banking sector.

On top of a good profitability, Intesa also has a strong capital position, with both factors being important for the bank’s attractive capital returns policy. Its CET1 ratio was 13.5% at the end of last June, a level that the bank sees as comfortable over the medium term, allowing it to distribute most annual earnings to shareholders. Its dividend policy is to distribute 70% of annual earnings through dividends and share buybacks, to maintain its capital ratio between 13-14% and return ‘excess capital’ to shareholders.

Its last annual dividend (related to 2024 earnings) was €0.341 per share, paid in two installments, leading to a dividend yield of around 6.2% at its current share price. This is much lower compared to when I last analyzed Intesa (it was yielding 11% at the time), showing that its shares no longer appear to be undervalued. While Intesa’s dividend yield is still attractive to income-oriented investors and is slightly above the average of the European banking sector, Intesa offered in the past a much higher dividend yield than peers, something that is no longer true.

Showing that Intesa is no longer undervalued following an impressive share price rally over the past couple of years, its valuation is nowadays much higher than it was in the past. As shown in the next graph, Intesa is trading at the top of its historical range over the past decade at around 1.4x book value (vs. 0.8x book value when I last analyzed it), a multiple that seems stretched as the bank’s earnings should be at a peak and momentum in the near future is not expected to be impressive.

Compared to peers, Intesa trades at a very similar valuation to its closest competitor, UniCredit (OTCPK:UNCRY), but it’s trading at a significant premium to Banco BPM (OTCPK:BNCZF) that is trading at only 1.17x book value. Compared to other European banks with a retail-oriented business model, such as ABN AMRO (OTCPK:AAVMY), Lloyds Banking Group (LYB), or ING Groep (ING), Intesa is also trading at a premium valuation, showing that its shares appear to be somewhat overvalued right now.

Conclusion

Intesa has reported a resilient operating performance during the first half of 2025, but operating momentum is not expected to be very bullish ahead, as rates are now a headwind for top-line growth and provisions for loan losses are likely to go up from low levels in recent quarters. Despite that, Intesa is trading at a high valuation compared to its own history and compared to peers, making its high dividend yield the best reason to hold its shares for the time being.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PennantPark Floating Rate Capital: I'm Buying This ~13% Yield Now
data.description:
PennantPark Floating Rate Capital (NYSE:PFLT) is one of the many BDCs that have been thrown into the discount rack by the market.

On a YTD basis, PFLT has lost about 18% of its market cap, which has resulted in a P/NAV of 0.77x. This is more or less in line with the sector-wide pricing considering that the sector median P/NAV is 0.78x. Because of the significant price drop, PFLT's dividend yield has jumped to 13.7%.

It is clear that the yield here is unsustainable and sooner or later will get cut. The same applies to the discount angle - the bulk of the discount is driven by systematic pressures, which, in my opinion, are justified. The base rates are already 25 basis points lower than going into 2025 and the consensus expectations indicate a series of near-term cuts. At the same time, the spreads continue to tighten amid subdued LBO activity and mushrooming supply of fresh capital earmarked to new private credit opportunities. On top of that the risks in the economy are mounting. It would be quite odd to argue against having some form of discounts in place.

Yet, PFLT seems to be one of the few BDC players that have been punished too hard. I think that the market has overlooked several nuances that separate PFLT from most of the peers. These nuances, which I will describe below, collectively make a strong case for relative outperformance.

Again, my expectation is not that PFLT's share price suddenly converges back to NAV or that the dividend at this level remains unchanged over, say, the next 2-3 quarters. Instead, the case boils down to profiting from stronger price recovery when the overall sector begins to move in the other (positive) direction, and capturing relatively de-risked current income streams, which even after the dividend cut would remain fairly attractive.

Thesis review

I will start with a rather broad element, which is something that sits right at the core of PFLT's value proposition and my bullish thesis.

The thing is that PFLT operates in the core middle market, where the average EBITDA generation for an investment company is between $10 million to $50 million. Many of the larger and well-known peers are operating in the upper middle market, where EBITDA generation for a typical company is significantly above $50 million. Plus, this is exactly the place, where the lion's share of new private credit capital formation takes place as the institutional giants simply have to operate with meaningful ticket size deals in order to avoid bloat and generate scale efficiencies.

Now, what this means is that the upper middle market segment has naturally become subject to unfavorable supply/demand dynamics. Namely, the bigger the supply, the stronger the pressure on spreads (i.e., profits). It is an Econ 101 thing - competition drives down prices/profits.

And here we can see how this looks in practice:

Another aspect that is worth appreciating is the difference in leverage. In the core middle market space, BDCs can also negotiate more favorable credit terms not only on price, but also on the leverage and coverage factor front.

So, by playing in the core middle market area, PFLT can enjoy higher spreads at lower risk.

In my humble opinion, this fact alone should drive premium pricing relative to the sector median; not a situation in which PFLT carries an even slightly lower multiple than P/NAV 0.77x (sector median).

If we look at what the picture looks like on the ground for PFLT, then I guess it would be only fair to conclude that the aforementioned substance really works in practice as well. I will give you three points:

  1. As of Q2 2025, PFLT's portfolio weighted average leverage ratio was 4.3x, and the weighted average interest coverage ratio was 2.5x. These are much stronger leverage and coverage metrics than, say, for widely known BDC blue chips: Ares Capital (NASDAQ:ARCC) and Blackstone Secured Lending (NYSE:BXSL).
  2. As of Q2 2025, PFLT had two investments under non-accruals, representing only 0.5% of portfolio market value. This is largely consistent with PFLT's historical average. More importantly, it is well-below sector average of about 2.5%.
  3. 90% of PFLT's portfolio is associated with the absolute safest loan investment category that is possible to obtain in BDC structures - i.e., senior secured first lien. The remaining 10% is linked to equity-like products (there is less than 1% in subordinated debt).

In my view, these aspects should warrant some kind of premium.

In the third point above I mentioned that about 10% of PFLT's portfolio consists of equity products. While there are some solid direct equity co-investment stakes, the bigger driver is the JV participation.

There is an existing JV - PSSL - which also invests in core middle markets. But then PFLT recently announced a formation of a new JV, partnering with Hamilton Lane. The company in Hamilton Lane has already committed $200 million of capital, which in conjunction with $300 million credit facility should secure about $0.5 billion of an additional asset pool in the JV (almost 25% of PFLT's current asset size).

The beauty here is that this funding (as of Q2) was planned to be deployed starting from September/October. Obviously, it takes time to lever up and deploy this kind of capital in the core middle market, but still, the takeaway is that going forward PFLT could benefit greatly from increased dividend flow from this newly formed JV.

Speaking of capital availability and fire power to accommodate growth, we have to factor in the excellent timing of PFLT's equity raise, which happened in Q1, right before the BDC sector-wide sell-off started. PFLT sourced $32 million at an average price of $11.31 per share (about 22% above the current share price).

Because of these dynamics, in a previous earnings call PFLT's management commented on the future dividend streams with the following:

Looking ahead, we are encouraged by the strong outlook for the remainder of the year and anticipate continued NII growth and full dividend coverage.

Considering that as of Q2 2025, the NII per share generation was $0.27 and the dividend per share at $0.31, we can imply that from the incremental capital deployment, PFLT expected to register ~$0.04 per share of growth in the quarterly NII result.

Sure, things have changed since then. The most immediate and perhaps the most material impact will come from lower base rates. And just because of this aspect alone, I would assume that PFLT cuts the current dividend. First, the NII per share is very unlikely to grow in a falling SOFR environment. Second, from the shareholder value creation perspective, it makes more sense to normalize the dividend and use that capital for buying back the shares at such an aggressively discounted price.

The bottom line

PFLT has been unfairly punished by the market.

As described in the main body above, it has much stronger fundamentals than the average BDC, but it is priced even slightly below the average.

On top of that, PFLT has embedded fire power to really expand the portfolio through the combination of timely raised equity (in Q1 before the BDC market tumbled) and a newly established/equitized JV that carries a notable scale in the context of PFLT's own portfolio.

While from the Management commentary we could understand that going into Q3 the expectation was to, in the short-term, grow the NII per share generation by at least $0.04, given how the base rates have evolved, I doubt that this will be possible. So, I am treating this initial expectation on NII per share growth as something that could help PFLT offset the forthcoming pressures, which is yet another luxury that many BDCs simply do not have.

In a nutshell, PFLT looks to me like an attractive high-yield case with high-probability price upside potential that should lead to a relative outperformance.
